title: Sudden tearing chest pain to the back with inferior ST elevation

chart.oneLiner: 58M with poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and CKD presents with sudden, severe, tearing chest pain radiating to the back that began 1 hour ago; the bedside EKG shows inferior ST elevation, raising an immediate fork between an inferior STEMI and a proximal aortic dissection occluding the right coronary.

opening: It came on all at once — the worst pain of my life, like something ripped in my chest and went right through to my back.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old man, in severe distress, restless, sweating. Frightened. Speaks in short sentences because of the pain.

HPI:
- Sudden, MAXIMAL-at-onset, severe chest pain ~1 hour ago, described as TEARING/RIPPING, radiating straight through to the back between the shoulder blades.
- Pain was at its worst immediately (not crescendo, not building). Migrated downward toward the abdomen and then the lower back over the last 30 minutes (only if asked about whether the pain moved).
- Felt briefly lightheaded and nearly fainted when the pain started (only if asked about dizziness/syncope).
- NO exertional trigger, NO relief with rest, NO pleuritic quality, NO cough/fever. Some shortness of breath but no wheeze.
- Known hypertension, frequently misses his medications and hasn't checked his BP in months (only mentions poor control if asked about adherence).
- Took a tablet for erections (sildenafil) yesterday evening (ONLY discloses this if asked specifically about any pills, supplements, or 'anything for sexual function' in the last day — embarrassed about it).

OLDCARTS: abrupt onset 1h ago, maximal immediately; retrosternal radiating to back then migrating to abdomen/lower back; constant; tearing/ripping; not positional or pleuritic; severity 10/10; associated near-syncope and dyspnea.

PMH: hypertension (poorly controlled), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease (stage 3, baseline creatinine ~1.5), gout, benign prostatic hyperplasia. No known coronary disease, no prior MI, no stents. FAMILY: father had an 'aneurysm' that burst and a brother with an early heart attack (only if asked).
MEDICATIONS (reveal the list only if asked what he takes; reveal nonadherence only if asked about adherence): lisinopril, amlodipine, and hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure (frequently nonadherent); metformin for diabetes; atorvastatin; allopurinol for gout; tamsulosin for the prostate; sildenafil taken yesterday evening for erectile dysfunction; takes over-the-counter ibuprofen 'a few times a week' for knee pain. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: current smoker ~40 pack-years, drinks 3-4 beers most evenings, no recreational drugs (denies cocaine if asked).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: diaphoretic, in severe pain, restless, anxious.
- Vitals: BP 192/64 in the RIGHT arm, 148/70 in the LEFT arm (>20 mmHg systolic differential); HR 58 (relatively bradycardic); wide pulse pressure on the right.
- JVP: mildly elevated if specifically assessed.
- Cardiac: regular but slow; soft early diastolic (blowing) murmur at the left sternal border if specifically auscultated (acute aortic regurgitation); no rub.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally; no crackles.
- Pulses: right radial fuller than left radial; right femoral diminished compared with left if checked; both lower-extremity pulses present but asymmetric.
- Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness. Abdomen: soft, mild diffuse discomfort, no rebound. Neuro: grossly intact, no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the medication nonadherence, the smoking, the alcohol, the NSAID use, the sildenafil, the family history, the near-syncope, or that the pain migrated unless the student specifically asks the matching question.
- The blood-pressure differential, the wide pulse pressure, the diastolic murmur, the asymmetric pulses, and the elevated JVP are revealed ONLY if the student measures BP in both arms, auscultates the heart, checks pulses, or assesses the JVP.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 10.9 (below his baseline of ~13.5 — a drop suggesting blood loss/sequestration into the false lumen), WBC 12.4 with neutrophil predominance, Plt 232.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.9, Cl 101, HCO3 18, BUN 38, Cr 2.3 (up from a baseline of ~1.5 — acute kidney injury on chronic kidney disease, suggesting renal malperfusion). Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 138 − (101 + 18) = 19 (elevated, ~7 above the upper-normal of 12).
labs.Lactate: 4.1 mmol/L (elevated) — accounts for the elevated anion-gap metabolic acidosis and points to malperfusion/hypoperfusion of a vascular bed.
labs.Troponin: Mildly elevated at 0.09 ng/mL (upper limit 0.04) and rising — a troponin leak that could reflect either a primary inferior STEMI OR right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal dissection; it does NOT settle the diagnosis.
labs.Ddimer: Markedly elevated (very sensitive but nonspecific; a normal value would have argued against dissection).
labs.ctangio: CT angiogram of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis: intimal flap originating in the ascending aorta extending into the arch and down the descending aorta — Stanford type A aortic dissection. The flap involves the right coronary ostium (explaining the inferior ST elevation and troponin) and the right renal artery (explaining the AKI); small pericardial effusion; no frank tamponade.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). Given the inferior ST elevation on the bedside EKG, explain why this is NOT a simple STEMI, and name three mechanisms or risk factors that predispose to your leading diagnosis.
steps.rubric: Aortic dissection (leading & CANT-MISS, 8; accepts dissecting aneurysm; the tearing maximal-at-onset pain, inter-arm BP differential, diastolic AR murmur, and asymmetric pulses point here despite the inferior ST elevation). Acute coronary syndrome / inferior STEMI (CANT-MISS, 4; a genuine competitor because the EKG shows inferior ST elevation and troponin is up — but proximal dissection can occlude the RCA and mimic it). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS, 2). Pericarditis/tamponade (core, 2). Esophageal rupture (bonus, 1). Names dissection and the discriminating features — tearing maximal-at-onset pain, inter-arm BP differential, AR murmur, asymmetric pulses (core, 4). Names THREE mechanisms/risk factors for dissection — e.g. chronic hypertension/shear stress on the media, cystic medial degeneration/connective-tissue disease (Marfan/Ehlers-Danlos), bicuspid aortic valve, atherosclerosis/age, cocaine/stimulant use, pregnancy, family history of aneurysm (core, 3). PENALTY: anchoring on STEMI and giving thrombolytics/anticoagulation without excluding dissection (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Aortic dissection (Stanford type A). The bedside EKG shows inferior ST elevation, but this is NOT a simple inferior STEMI: a proximal (type A) dissection can extend into the right coronary ostium and occlude it, producing inferior ST elevation and a troponin leak. The features that point to dissection over primary ACS are the abrupt MAXIMAL-at-onset tearing pain radiating to the back and migrating, the inter-arm systolic BP differential (>20 mmHg), the new early-diastolic aortic-regurgitation murmur, asymmetric pulses, and the markedly elevated D-dimer. Must-not-miss competitors: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior STEMI (the dangerous look-alike) and Pulmonary embolism; also consider pericarditis with effusion/tamponade. Three mechanisms/risk factors for dissection: (1) chronic poorly-controlled hypertension causing repetitive shear stress on the aortic media; (2) cystic medial degeneration / connective-tissue disease (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, bicuspid aortic valve); (3) atherosclerosis with advancing age — others include cocaine/stimulant use, pregnancy, and a family history of aneurysm (he has both).

criticalActions.item: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior STEMI (genuine competitor)

coreActions.item: Names dissection and the discriminating features — tearing maximal-at-onset pain, inter-arm BP differential, AR murmur, asymmetric pulses
Names three mechanisms/risk factors for dissection (HTN/shear, cystic medial degeneration/connective tissue disease, atherosclerosis/age, cocaine, family history)

penalties.item: Anchors on STEMI and gives thrombolytics/anticoagulation without excluding dissection

mustNotMiss: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior STEMI (genuine competitor)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What workup do you want? (And what must you check at the bedside before committing?)
steps.rubric: Bilateral arm BP / pulse exam (CANT-MISS,3). EKG (already shows inferior ST elevation — interpret in the context of the chest exam, do NOT reflexively activate the cath lab) (core,2). CXR (core,2). CT angiogram of chest/abdomen/pelvis (CANT-MISS, 4; accepts CTA or TEE if too unstable for CT). Troponin (core,1). CBC + type and crossmatch blood (core,1). BMP/renal function and lactate to assess malperfusion (core,1). Bedside echo for AR/effusion/tamponade (bonus,1). PENALTY: anticoagulating/thrombolysing before excluding dissection (-3); giving a nitrate/PDE5-interaction vasodilator before checking medication history (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Check both arms' BP and all four pulses at the bedside FIRST. Order: EKG (already shows inferior ST elevation — interpret it alongside the inter-arm differential and AR murmur rather than reflexively activating the cath lab); CXR; CBC with type and crossmatch; BMP with renal function; lactate; troponin; D-dimer; CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis as the definitive test (TEE if too unstable to travel). A bedside echo can quickly assess for AR, pericardial effusion, and tamponade.

coreActions.item: EKG interpreted in context (do not reflexively activate cath lab)

bonusActions.item: Bedside echo for AR / effusion / tamponade

penalties.item: Anticoagulating or thrombolysing before excluding dissection
Giving a nitrate/PDE5-interacting vasodilator before checking medication history

optionalTests: EKG interpreted in context (do not reflexively activate cath lab)
contraindicatedTests: Anticoagulating or thrombolysing before excluding dissection
orderSensitiveActions: Anticoagulating or thrombolysing before excluding dissection

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they fit the clinical picture.
steps.rubric: Findings = inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal change in aVL and right-sided ST elevation (V4R) of right-ventricular involvement; the relative bradycardia fits inferior/RV ischemia. CRUCIAL: recognizes this can be RIGHT-CORONARY malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection, NOT necessarily a primary thrombotic STEMI — so anticoagulation/thrombolysis is contraindicated until dissection is excluded (7); reads the inferior STEMI pattern systematically (4); recognizes RV involvement / reciprocal change (2). PENALTY: calling it a simple STEMI and reflexively thrombolysing/anticoagulating without considering dissection (-3).
keyFindings: inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal change in aVL
right-ventricular involvement (V4R) — and the possibility this is RCA malperfusion from a proximal dissection, not a primary STEMI
criticalFindings: Inferior ST elevation that may reflect right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection — not a thrombus to anticoagulate.
commonMistakes: reading the inferior ST elevation as a simple thrombotic STEMI and giving anticoagulation/thrombolytics without excluding dissection
steps.idealAnswer: Inferior ST elevation in II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in aVL, plus right-sided changes (V4R) of right-ventricular involvement; the relative bradycardia is consistent with inferior/RV ischemia. The key integration: in a patient with tearing pain, an inter-arm BP differential, and an AR murmur, this inferior ST elevation may be RIGHT-CORONARY malperfusion from a proximal (type A) aortic dissection rather than a primary thrombotic STEMI — so thrombolysis and anticoagulation are CONTRAINDICATED until dissection is excluded by CT.

criticalActions: Inferior ST elevation that may reflect right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection — not a thrombus to anticoagulate.
coreActions: inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal change in aVL
right-ventricular involvement (V4R) — consider RCA malperfusion from dissection, not a primary STEMI

penalties.label: calls it a simple STEMI and thrombolyses/anticoagulates without excluding dissection

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = WIDENED MEDIASTINUM with an enlarged aortic knob (and a possible small left pleural effusion). CREDIT: identifies the widened mediastinum (6); links it to suspected aortic dissection (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the widened mediastinum (-3).
keyFindings: links it to suspected aortic dissection
commonMistakes: calling the film normal / missing the widened mediastinum
steps.idealAnswer: Widened mediastinum with an enlarged aortic knob and a possible small left pleural effusion — highly suggestive of aortic dissection in this clinical context.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Widened mediastinum; enlarged aortic knob.

coreActions.item: links it to suspected aortic dissection

penalties.item: calling the film normal / missing the widened mediastinum

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the CT findings, commit to a diagnosis. Explain why the location matters, how the inferior ST elevation and the acute kidney injury are explained by ONE unifying process, and name the discriminating test and any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Stanford type A aortic dissection (accepts ascending aortic dissection) is the unifying diagnosis. Recognizes type A involves the ascending aorta and is a SURGICAL emergency (vs type B, usually medical). Explains the ONE process accounts for the multi-system picture: RCA-ostium involvement → inferior ST elevation + troponin leak (a malperfusion STEMI, NOT a thrombotic one); right-renal-artery involvement → AKI on CKD with lactic acidosis from malperfusion; intimal flap → acute AR murmur and pericardial effusion; falling Hgb from sequestration/leak. Names the discriminating test (CT angiography, or TEE if unstable) and residual uncertainty (dynamic vs static malperfusion, tamponade risk from the effusion). Notes complications — acute AR, tamponade, coronary/renal/mesenteric/limb malperfusion, rupture.
steps.idealAnswer: Stanford type A aortic dissection — the flap arises in the ascending aorta, which makes it a SURGICAL emergency (type B descending dissections are usually managed medically). One unifying process explains everything: the flap reaches the right coronary ostium, causing right-coronary MALPERFUSION that produced the inferior ST elevation and the troponin leak (a malperfusion 'STEMI', not a thrombotic one — which is exactly why thrombolysis/anticoagulation would have been catastrophic); it reaches the right renal artery, causing the acute kidney injury on CKD and the lactic/anion-gap acidosis from malperfusion; the proximal flap disrupts the aortic valve, giving the acute aortic-regurgitation murmur and the small pericardial effusion; and blood sequestered into the false lumen explains the fall in hemoglobin. The discriminating test is CT angiography (or TEE if too unstable to travel). Residual uncertainty: whether the malperfusion is dynamic or static, and the risk that the pericardial effusion progresses to tamponade. Complications to watch: acute AR, tamponade, coronary/renal/mesenteric/limb malperfusion, and free rupture.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Include disposition: is this patient ever safe to discharge, and what level of care and transfer does he need?
steps.rubric: Per MGH Acute Aortic Syndromes: rate control FIRST with IV beta blocker (esmolol/labetalol), target HR <60 (CANT-MISS,6); THEN BP control to SBP 100-120, adding a vasodilator (nitroprusside) ONLY after beta blockade (4); STAT cardiothoracic surgery consult — type A is emergent surgery, type B is medical (CANT-MISS,5); pain control with opioids (2); large-bore IV access + type & crossmatch, treat the falling Hgb (2); explicit safe-disposition statement — this is NEVER dischargeable, admit to ICU and transfer emergently to a center with cardiac surgery if not available locally; do NOT send to the cath lab (3). Holds/avoids nephrotoxins and dose-adjusts for AKI; notes the recent sildenafil contraindicates nitrates and cautions nitroprusside (bonus,1). PENALTY: vasodilator before beta blockade → reflex tachycardia/increased shear (-3); anticoagulation/thrombolytics or cath-lab activation for the 'STEMI' (-4); discharging or under-triaging the patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: This is an emergency and the patient is NEVER safe to discharge. Control heart rate FIRST with an IV beta blocker (e.g. esmolol or labetalol, target HR <60) to reduce dP/dt and aortic wall shear, THEN control blood pressure to SBP 100-120, adding nitroprusside ONLY after beta blockade (a vasodilator first would cause reflex tachycardia and worse shear). Note he took sildenafil yesterday — avoid nitrates and use vasodilators cautiously. Opioid pain control. Two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, and resuscitate the falling hemoglobin. STAT cardiothoracic surgery consult: a Stanford type A dissection is an emergent surgical repair (a type B would be managed medically). Do NOT activate the cath lab or give anticoagulation/thrombolytics for the inferior ST elevation — it is malperfusion, not thrombus. Hold nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, his ACE inhibitor) and dose-adjust for the AKI. Disposition: admit to the ICU; if there is no on-site cardiac surgery, arrange emergent transfer to a center that has it. There is no safe-discharge pathway here.

criticalActions.item: IV beta blocker for rate control (HR<60)

coreActions.item: Blood pressure control to SBP 100-120 (nitroprusside only after beta blocker)
Large-bore IV access, type & crossmatch, resuscitate falling Hgb
Safe-disposition: never dischargeable — admit ICU and emergently transfer to a cardiac-surgery center; do NOT send to cath lab

bonusActions.item: Hold nephrotoxins / dose-adjust for AKI; avoid nitrates given recent sildenafil
Type A surgical vs type B medical distinction

penalties.item: Anticoagulation, thrombolytics, or cath-lab activation for the 'STEMI'

requiredActions: IV beta blocker for rate control (HR<60)
optionalActions: Blood pressure control to SBP 100-120 (nitroprusside only after beta blocker)
Large-bore IV access, type & crossmatch, resuscitate falling Hgb
Safe-disposition: never dischargeable — admit ICU and emergently transfer to a cardiac-surgery center; do NOT send to cath lab
Type A surgical vs type B medical distinction
Hold nephrotoxins / dose-adjust for AKI; avoid nitrates given recent sildenafil

steps.disposition: Emergent; ICU then OR / transfer to cardiac-surgery center

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Inferior STEMI with right ventricular involvement: ST elevation in II, III, aVF with reciprocal ST depression in aVL and ST elevation in V4R; relative bradycardia. In this patient the inferior ST elevation reflects right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection, not a primary thrombotic STEMI.
ekg.verify: Use LITFL ECG case 001 (inferior STEMI with RV infarction). Self-host with appropriate credit.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: regular but slow; soft early-diastolic (blowing) murmur at the left sternal border if specifically auscultated (acute aortic regurgitation); no rub.
Pulses: right radial fuller than left radial; right femoral diminished compared with left; both lower-extremity pulses present but asymmetric.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Pulses symmetric in all four extremities.

physicalExamMappings.finding: JVP mildly elevated (raises concern for a pericardial effusion / early tamponade).
General: diaphoretic, in severe pain, restless, anxious.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58M with poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, CKD, and significant polypharmacy presents with sudden, severe, tearing chest pain radiating to the back that began 1 hour ago. The bedside EKG shows inferior ST elevation, creating a genuine fork between a primary inferior STEMI and a Stanford type A aortic dissection that has extended into the right coronary ostium. The diagnosis is Aortic dissection: one unifying process accounts for the inferior ST elevation and troponin leak (RCA malperfusion), the acute kidney injury and lactic/anion-gap acidosis (renal-artery malperfusion), the acute aortic-regurgitation murmur and pericardial effusion (proximal flap), and the falling hemoglobin (false-lumen sequestration). It is reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam (inter-arm BP differential, AR murmur, asymmetric pulses), interpreting the integrated EKG and CXR in context, and crucially WITHHOLDING anticoagulation/thrombolysis until CT angiography confirms the dissection.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular but slow; soft early-diastolic (blowing) murmur at the left sternal border if specifically auscultated (acute aortic regurgitation); no rub.
Pulses: right radial fuller than left radial; right femoral diminished compared with left; both lower-extremity pulses present but asymmetric.
JVP mildly elevated (raises concern for a pericardial effusion / early tamponade).
General: diaphoretic, in severe pain, restless, anxious.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the bradycardia and the wide pulse pressure.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (nonadherence, sildenafil, family aneurysm) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam — bilateral arm BP, cardiac auscultation, pulses, JVP — to reveal the dissection signs.
Build a differential that holds aortic dissection and inferior STEMI as competing leaders, and resist anchoring on the EKG.
Order an order-sensitive workup, interpret the integrated inferior-STEMI EKG and the widened-mediastinum CXR in context, and confirm with CT angiography.

Commit to Stanford type A aortic dissection, explain the unifying malperfusion physiology, and manage with rate-then-pressure control, STAT surgery, and ICU/transfer disposition without anticoagulation.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on the inferior STEMI and activating the cath lab / giving anticoagulation or thrombolytics
Missing the inter-arm BP differential and asymmetric pulses by not examining them
managementPearls: IV beta blocker for rate control (HR<60) before any vasodilator
STAT cardiothoracic surgery consult for a type A dissection
Never dischargeable — ICU admission and emergent transfer to a cardiac-surgery center
criticalInterpretationItems: Inferior ST elevation that may reflect right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection — not a thrombus to anticoagulate.
criticalManagementItems: IV beta blocker for rate control (HR<60)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal change in aVL and right-ventricular involvement — possible RCA malperfusion from dissection.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Inferior ST elevation that may reflect right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal aortic dissection — not a thrombus to anticoagulate.
imageInterpretationPearls: Inferior ST elevation is not always a thrombotic STEMI — in a patient with tearing pain and an inter-arm BP differential, consider right-coronary malperfusion from a proximal dissection before anticoagulating.
Read every study systematically before committing, and integrate it with the bedside exam.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's pain and fear
Asks permission before sensitive questions (adherence, sexual-function medication, drug use)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
